7 min read
Just now

--

I put GPT models head-to-head in Doom.
Doom Agent Arena is a real time benchmark where AI agents control Doom players and fight each other across multiple rounds.
This blog post explains how I built Doom Agent Arena with Codex, then compared model performance, execution efficiency, and strategic behavior. We will see how resources played a critical role and how models recognized winning patterns over repeated rounds.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1: Doom Agent Arena: Two model-controlled agents compete in Doom using high level MCP intents.1. How I built Doom Agent Arena
Doom Agent Arena is a model-vs-model environment where two AI agents control opposing players through MCP tools. I was interested in understanding how models interpret a dynamic environment, choose actions while weighing consequences, recover from failed plans, and adapt their strategy over time.
Each agent runs in a separate Codex session with a predefined prompt containing instructions and constraints. The Doom Agent Arena MCP server lets agents observe the Doom game state, like map and resources, and submit high-level plans for Doom to execute.
Agents then use those tools to submit a route, and planning note, which Doom executes through its autopilot and updates the game state.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 2: Control loop: agents observe game state, submit route-based plans through MCP, and Doom executes the low-level actions.Designing Agent Control
The initial MVP exposed too many low-level MCP controls. For example, for the agent to control the character movements on the map, it would need to use commands such as strafe_left, turn_right, shoot and look_at_enemy, making the benchmark noisy because performance was dominated by how quickly agents issued small control actions instead of quality of strategies. This was most noticeable in fights where faster models routinely won by simply calling shoot commands more quickly.
Instead, I tried hierarchical MCP intents, where the model selected from a small action space before choosing sub-actions (decision-tree style). This reduced low-level control, but made plans difficult to interpret because intent had to be inferred from action labels than explicit reasoning. And because each action was fixed, an ‘explore’ call would make the agent spin while moving forward, even if there was a wall. This led to agents unable to properly navigate or adapt its strategy based on the map because of the limited action options.
The best design I found was a single route submission of coordinates, along with an objective, reasoning, and public plan note. This kept the action format simple, while still allowing behavior like kiting, baiting, retreating to collect health packs, pushing toward the shotgun, or flanking.
Example route submission:
{participant: player_1,
objective: kite shotgun user from long range,
route: [Q26, Q22],
reasoning: Opponent likely has shotgun, so I should avoid close range and use a long corridor,
plan_note: Back away through Q-lane, keep distance, and shoot only in line of sight}Using a single action route submission halved latency. Doom handled shooting and other low-level execution, leaving models to plan routes between fights. This shifted the benchmark from tool-calling speed to plan quality.
Building a Balanced Map
I first tried using Doom’s E1M8 star-shaped map, which had drawbacks: the fighting area was too small and players spawned across from each other, leaving little room to develop strategies. Another issue was side spawn bias: player 1 spawn had a 75% win-rate, indicating an advantage from map elevation and better wall cover.
To resolve this, I created a custom map that encouraged route planning and resource control, without being overly cluttered. The maze style encouraged strategic exploration through various routes. Shotguns were located towards the center, and health packs were scattered evenly throughout the map to reward effective navigation and resource management.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 3: Custom map with opposite spawns, central shotguns (S), and health packs (+)Benchmark Design Summary
Doom Agent Arena benchmark evaluates strategic reasoning instead of low-level control. The custom map reduced side spawn bias, added multiple route options, and placed resources so agents can plan around them.
Each player kept the same codex session across rounds to study changes in reasoning, strategy and planning speed. Agents received no opponent information, unless they were visible in-game. I also increased character health from 100 to 300, giving agents more time for meaningful fights, retreats, and health-pack decisions while reducing lucky kills.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 1: Benchmark design choices2. Results and Findings
GPT-5.5 was the strongest overall model, finishing with a 67% win score and the highest average damage difference. Each model-matchup was 10 rounds per side, with a fresh Codex session for each side.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 2: Leaderboard across 60 matches per model. Draws count as half-wins when computing scores.In head-to-head results, GPT-5.3-codex-spark was GPT-5.5’s closest matchup, with GPT-5.5 winning 57%. GPT-5.4-mini performed weakest overall.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 4: Head-to-head score (%) heatmap across model pairings.Finding 1: Smart Resource Use Was the Clearest Winning Signal
Resource control was the clearest winning signal. GPT-5.5 collected 4× more health pickups than the next-highest model, using them to escape and recover. However, more pickups didn’t guarantee wins: GPT-5.4 collected the most shotguns, but GPT-5.5 still performed better head-to-head.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 3: Resource control summary for shotgun and health-packs.Finding 2: More Plans and faster Execution did not Guarantee more wins
While GPT-5.3-codex-spark submitted the most plans, 27.1% were invalid (walls, blocked cells, routes out of bounds). It finished third overall, showing that more planning did not mean better planning.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 4: Plan execution metrics across models.Across models I noticed a decrease in decision latency and invalid plans as rounds progressed, suggesting agents become more familiar with valid routes and the game map structure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 5: Mean decision latency over rounds by model.Finding 3: Submitted Plans Revealed Model Tactics and Playstyles
What I found most interesting were the tactical plans that the models came up with.
Over time, agents would develop strategies such as baiting opponents, stealing health packs, fighting outside shotgun range, and flanking previous enemy positions. This showed that they were using memory and context, not just reacting to the current state.
GPT-5.5 also used kiting, running away while attacking, which connected weapon range, movement, and positioning in one plan.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 5: Examples of submitted agent plansModels also showed cross-round thinking by leveraging learnings from the past and planning around future contingencies. GPT-5.5 had the highest future-contingency rate at 77.9%, while GPT-5.4 had the highest overall temporal planning rate at 91.1%.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Table 6: Temporal planning signals in accepted public plan.Finding 4: Longer Runs Revealed Creative Ways Around the Intended Protocol
Aside from the benchmark protocol, I tested longer sessions of 30+ rounds. In some cases, agents would create temporary controller scripts that automated route submissions from previously successful plans that led to wins, such as routing to health packs when health dropped below a threshold.
My interpretation is that this may be an example of benchmark gaming where agents realized that hard-coded controller scripts could reduce inference time and outperform live planning, bypassing the intended structured JSON planning protocol.
This shows that when agents are given a goal, they may break rules or find creative ways to circumnavigate the intended constraints if doing so helps them win.
3. Conclusion
Doom Agent Arena shows that model-vs-model game environments reveal planning behaviors ordinary benchmarks may miss. High performing agents control resources, avoid unfavorable engagements, repeat successful plans, and adapt over time, while faster planning and lower error rates do not translate into wins.
Doom Agent Arena was built within Rootly AI Labs, a fellow-led community building open-source tools and prototypes for AI in incident management and reliability.
Although built in a game setting, the benchmark targets reliability-relevant LLM skills: interpreting dynamic environments, reasoning about consequences, recovering from failed plans, and adapting under time or cost constraints. We wrote about the correlation between both worlds in this article.
The most exciting part for me was seeing that even in a simple game setup, models could reveal meaningful differences in planning style, resource awareness, and adaptation.
Stay Tuned for the full paper!
